title: Iran Gas Deal Expires With No Extension Talks, Iraq-Pipeline Race Heats Up, Turkish Navy Returns to Latakia
subhead: Turkey's long-term natural gas contract with Iran expired in July with no negotiations underway, while Ankara seals a one-year Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline deal with Baghdad and Turkish warships dock in Syria's Latakia for the first time since 2011.

sections.heading: Iran Gas Contract Expires — No Extension Talks Underway
sections.body:
Turkish Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar confirmed that Ankara's long-term natural gas supply contract with Iran expired in July, and no negotiations on an extension are currently underway. The contract covered 9.6 billion cubic meters per year of Iranian gas deliveries, though actual flows frequently fell short — Turkey imported only 7.6 bcm from Iran last year, accounting for 13% of total gas imports.

Bayraktar told reporters at the Antalya Diplomacy Forum that Turkey 'might need this gas pipeline or the gas flow from Iran for the security of supply,' but attributed the absence of talks to the ongoing Iran war. 'We haven't started a negotiation during the current circumstances in the region,' he said, according to Turkiye Today.

Turkey is diversifying its supply, including a new 10-year LNG import license for Russian gas via BOTAS, though Bayraktar confirmed no Russian LNG imports have actually started. Russian pipeline gas via BlueStream and TurkStream still accounts for roughly 35% of Turkey's gas mix. The contract's expiry ahead of winter demand adds urgency to supply security planning.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran gas contract lapsing mid-war removes a significant supply source right before winter. Turkey's attempt to pivot to Russian LNG and alternative suppliers will test whether it can maintain energy hub ambitions without Iranian volumes.

sections.heading: Kirkuk-Ceyhan Pipeline Extended One Year as Iraq Seeks Hormuz Bypass
sections.body:
Turkey has agreed to continue transit oil shipments from Iraq through the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline for one year, according to Upstream Online, after Baghdad requested an extension. The existing agreement was set to expire at the end of July, and Iraq is heavily dependent on the route — roughly two-thirds of its crude exports had been halted by the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

But the terms are contested. Turkey opposed extending the agreement under its current conditions, a senior Turkish official told Reuters in mid-June. Iraq is now seeking a nearly four-fold capacity increase on the pipeline, Daily Sabah reported, and has also signed a memorandum of understanding with Syria to restore the long-idled Iraq-Syria pipeline (capacity: 700,000 bpd), with Chevron reportedly leading the consortium for its rehabilitation.

Iraqi Oil Minister Bassim Khudair said a feasibility study is underway for a pipeline from Basra and Kirkuk to Mediterranean ports in Ceyhan and Syria's Baniyas, with a possible extension to Aqaba. A second pipeline bypassing the Kurdish region is undergoing final testing and could be operational within days, he added.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz blockade has turned Kirkuk-Ceyhan into Iraq's lifeline, and Turkey is leveraging that dependency to renegotiate terms. The one-year extension is a stopgap — the real play is whether Turkey can parlay its transit role into a permanent Mediterranean energy corridor.

sections.heading: Turkish Navy Docks in Latakia — First Since Syrian Civil War
sections.body:
Turkish warships visited Syria's Latakia Port on July 13, marking the first Turkish naval visit since the start of Syria's civil war in 2011. The frigate TCG Istanbul carried Naval Forces Commander Adm. Ercument Tatlioglu to Latakia for talks with Syrian Navy Commander Brig. Gen. Mohammed Al-Saud, Turkiye's Defense Ministry announced.

Turkish Ambassador to Damascus Nuh Yilmaz said cooperation and solidarity between Turkey and Syria are 'expanding in the naval field.' The visit follows Assad's ouster in late 2024 and the formation of a transitional administration under President Ahmad al-Sharaa in January.

The naval call signals how far Ankara-Damascus relations have shifted in under a year — from backing opposite sides in a decade-long war to coordinating port calls and naval cooperation. It also reflects Turkey's effort to consolidate its Mediterranean posture as it builds energy transit infrastructure and deepens ties with Syria's new government.
sections.whyItMatters: A Turkish naval commander visiting Latakia aboard a warship would have been unthinkable two years ago. It marks Turkey's strategic re-entry into Syria's security architecture under the post-Assad order — and a signal to both Moscow and Tehran that Ankara has a seat at the table.

sections.heading: S-400 Sale Stalls — Russia Holds Veto, Israel Raises Objections
sections.body:
The reported S-400 transfer to a Gulf state remains unconfirmed. Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov acknowledged that Moscow and Ankara are in contact over the system's fate but called the matter 'extremely sensitive,' according to Reuters. Analyst Thrasyvulos Eftychidis told parapolitika.gr that Russia 'does not accept their return, nor does it agree to their transfer to another country without its consent' — authorization that has not been granted.

Hürriyet columnist Abdulkadir Selvi reported on July 10 that the S-400s had been sold to a Gulf country (UAE or Qatar), with an announcement expected the same day. No announcement followed, and no capital has confirmed the sale since.

Israel adds another layer of resistance. Prime Minister Netanyahu phoned Trump to object to F-35 sales to Turkey, citing Erdogan's rhetoric against Israel. Greece's deputy foreign minister Alexandra Papadopoulou told parliament that the US 'has made no commitment' to supply Turkey with F-35s. Former Turkish Defence Minister Hulusi Akar visited Washington for talks with House Armed Services Committee Chair Mike Rogers on resolving the S-400 issue, Indicator reported.

Meanwhile, the FDD's Sinan Ciddi argues that moving the S-400 to the Gulf 'solves Turkey's problem, not Washington's' — it relocates Russian intelligence collection next to Al Udeid, the largest US base in the Middle East, during an active conflict with Iran. Under US law, lifting CAATSA requires a presidential notification to Congress that the S-400 is non-operational, gone from Turkey, and that Ankara pledges no similar Russian deals, with a 90-day review period.
sections.whyItMatters: Three capitals can still kill this deal: Moscow (contractual veto), Jerusalem (qualitative military edge objection), and Washington (congressional review). The promised announcement never came, and the longer Russia delays consent, the more leverage it retains over Ankara.

sections.heading: Leonardo-Baykar Drone Venture Gets Conditional Green Light
sections.body:
Italy's government has given conditional approval to the joint venture between state-controlled defense group Leonardo and Turkey's Baykar to produce unmanned aerial vehicles, Reuters reported on June 17. The approval comes with export restrictions designed to safeguard Italian national security interests.

The venture builds on a successful demonstration: an Italian M-346 jet trainer controlled a Baykar Kizilelma drone in flight during a loyal wingman trial at Baykar's Çorlu test center. Leonardo said the two aircraft 'undertook a series of missions to test algorithms, tactics and procedures' for crewed-uncrewed teaming, Defense News reported. New tests with increasing complexity are planned.

The partnership is significant for both sides. Baykar gains access to Leonardo's advanced avionics and European market channels; Leonardo gets Baykar's low-cost drone production and a foothold in the loyal wingman space as the FCAS Next Generation Fighter component was terminated in June after Franco-German disagreements. Turkey's defense exports have more than doubled their global share, from 0.9% in 2016-2020 to 1.8%, making it the world's 11th-largest arms supplier, Geopolitical Futures reports.
sections.whyItMatters: The Leonardo-Baykar venture is a rare example of a NATO member formally partnering with Turkey's defense industry despite ongoing political friction. It gives Baykar a European manufacturing base and Leonardo a cost-effective drone platform — but export restrictions signal Italy's caution about technology transfer.

sections.heading: Opposition Crisis Deepens as Court Ouster Fuels Democratic Backsliding Concerns
sections.body:
The crisis in Turkey's main opposition party shows no signs of resolution. An appeals court annulled the CHP's 2023 leadership election, deposing popular leader Özgür Özel and reinstating Kemal Kılıçdaroğlu — the 77-year-old veteran who led the party to five consecutive election defeats — as interim leader.

According to The Conversation, the court ruled the CHP's 38th congress was 'void from the beginning,' overturning an earlier ruling that had rejected allegations of irregularities. Human Rights Watch called İmamoğlu's separate prosecution part of a broad strategy to 'weaponise the criminal justice system' against the CHP, with the Istanbul mayor facing prosecutors seeking up to 2,352 years in prison on corruption charges.

Özel has accused the AKP of pursuing an agenda to 'eliminate its rivals,' and the ruling fits what Freedom House describes as a pattern of using institutional tools to weaken or co-opt political opponents. Erdoğan cannot run again under current term limits unless he calls early elections or changes the constitution — moves his coalition currently lacks votes for. The CHP's record 2024 local election wins made the crackdown more urgent from the government's perspective.
sections.whyItMatters: The court-ordered ouster of a popularly elected opposition leader — reversing a democratic mandate — is the sharpest judicial intervention in party politics in years. It leaves the CHP internally fractured ahead of 2028 elections and signals that institutional constraints on Erdoğan's power remain weak.

bottomLine: Turkey's energy landscape shifted this week as the Iran gas contract expired without extension talks, forcing Ankara to lean harder on Russian pipeline gas and Iraqi crude transit just as it finalizes a one-year Kirkuk-Ceyhan deal with Baghdad. The Turkish Navy's return to Latakia — the first since 2011 — marks a strategic realignment with post-Assad Syria that pairs with growing energy corridor ambitions. Meanwhile, the S-400 transfer remains stuck: no Gulf buyer has been confirmed, Russia holds a contractual veto, Israel is lobbying against the F-35 sale, and US congressional review looms. The opposition crisis continues to deepen with no resolution in sight, and Baykar's partnership with Leonardo signals that Turkey's defense industry is finding European partners despite political headwinds.
sources: Turkiye Today, Indicator.gr, Aviation Week, Reuters, 19FortyFive, Horizon Weekly, Defense News, Geopolitical Futures, The Conversation, Upstream Online, Yahoo/WSJ, Seeking Alpha, Daily Sabah, Parapolitika.gr, Human Rights Watch, Freedom House
